title-group.article-title: A Case Report on Alveolar Rhabdomyosarcoma in an Indigenous Dog

aff.institution: Chattogram Veterinary and Animal Sciences University
Chattogram Veterinary and Animal Sciences University
Chattogram Veterinary and Animal Sciences University

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: A 12 years‐old, male indigenous dog was presented to Shahedul Alam Qaderi Teaching Veterinary Hospital (SAQTVH) in Chattogram, Bangladesh with a large tumour on its right front leg. The histopathological findings of the tumour were highly suggestive of an alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma (ARMS). The tumour was surgically removed from the dog. While there had been no recurrence for 5 months, the patient died 5 months post‐operatively. The cause of the death and presence of metastasis could not be confirmed as follow‐up investigations were declined by the owner. A histopathological study showed poorly differentiated neoplastic mononuclear cells forming nests in an alveolar pattern. Despite that the limited resources prevented myogenin staining for diagnostic confirmation, the well‐contained, defined appearance of the tumour supports the diagnosis of ARMS. This report highlights the challenges in diagnosing and treating ARMS in indigenous dogs and emphasizes the need for further research and diagnostic markers to improve detection and management.

A 12‐year‐old male indigenous dog was presented with a large tumour on its right front leg. The tumour was surgically excised under general anaesthesia, and histopathology was performed. The histopathological findings revealed poorly differentiated neoplastic mononuclear cells forming nests in an alveolar pattern. The tumour's well‐defined appearance strongly suggested that it was an alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma (ARMS), even though myogenin staining was required for confirmation.

fn.p: Funding: The authors received no specific funding for this work.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) is an unusual malignant tumour primarily affecting young individuals and originating in skeletal muscle. It poses a diagnostic challenge in both human and veterinary medicine due to its infrequency and wide variation of macroscopic and microscopic presentations (Caserto 2013). While RMS typically emerges in skeletal muscle, it can also develop in non‐striated muscle organs such as the urinary bladder, uterus, and cervix (Bae et al. 2007). In dogs, RMS has been observed in various locations, including the pharynx, larynx, cardiac muscle, gingiva, urinary bladder, larger omentum, urethra, skin, and trachea (Brockus and Myers 2004).

RMS in animals, especially in dogs, is a relatively rare type of tumour that exhibits considerable variation in its appearance, histology, and cellular characteristics. Common histological subtypes encompass embryonal RMS, alveolar RMS, and botryoid RMS, with pleomorphic RMS being less frequent. Due to their rarity, the diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment of these malignancies are challenging. Botryoid RMS is the most prevalent subtype, followed by embryonal, alveolar, and the less common pleomorphic subtypes, as per data from veterinary case reports (Gombert et al. 2020). According to Caserto (2013), 63% (n = 39/62; age was not given in two cases) of canine RMS (apart from laryngeal RMS) occurs in dogs under the age of 2 years. The remaining 37% are distributed among animals aged 3–15 years (n = 9/62; 15%), 7–10 years (n = 7/62; 11%), and >10 years (n = 7/62; 11%). Eighty‐nine percent (n = 55/62) of cases involve dogs under the age of 10 years.

The diverse phenotypes and cellular structures of RMS make their detection and classification challenging, which might explain their limited presence in the literature. There are three variants of embryonal RMS based on cytological characteristics. Myotubular RMS, this variant is characterized by multinucleated strap cells that produce myotubes (Caserto 2013); rhabdomyoblastic RMS, this variant features an abundance of round to polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm (Parham 2001); spindle‐cell RMS, this is a recently identified, uncommon subtype where thin spindyloid myoblast cells typically form bundles and myxoid stroma (Cooper and Valentine 2002). Botryoid RMS mainly appears as a polypoid, grape‐like mass primarily in the urinary bladder that has histological characteristics including strap cells and undifferentiated rhabdomyoblasts inside a myxoid matrix (Kobayashi et al. 2004). Histologically, alveolar RMS can be divided into two categories: solid and classic, the classic type is characterized by small, poorly differentiated cells with little cytoplasm and the formation of an alveolar pattern. The solid variant consists of tiny round neoplastic cells separated by thin fibrous septa; its histologic morphology is similar to embryonal RMS (Pîrvu, Nicolae, and Militaru 2023). Pleomorphic RMS, the least common RMS form in humans and typically found in adult dogs, is characterized by spindle cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, an absence of embryonal or alveolar patterns, unusual mitotic figures, and a high degree of pleomorphism (Caserto 2013).

In veterinary medicine, achieving a prognosis and proper diagnosis for canine RMS is challenging due to its rarity and frequent misinterpretation. Their significant variety in phenotype, location, age of onset, and cellular architecture makes diagnosis and classification challenging, which can lead to frequent misinterpretations (Caserto 2013). The prognostic significance of canine RMS remains a challenge for veterinary professionals due to limited follow‐up and post‐mortem evaluation. In this case study, based on clinical sign, the case was suspected that it would be a connective tissue tumour. There has no published research on RMS in indigenous dogs in Bangladesh. Accordingly, the case was investigated and analyzed through histopathological study.

This study aims to explore the histological and cytological characteristics of canine RMS, with a specific focus on the diagnostic and prognostic aspects of this rare condition in veterinary medicine with its diverse manifestations.

sec.title: Materials and Methods
sec.p: A 12‐year‐old, male indigenous dog was brought to Shahedul Alam Qaderi Teaching Veterinary Hospital (SAQTVH) in Chattogram, Bangladesh due to the growth of a sizable mass on its right front leg near the elbow joint. The mass felt hard and could not be moved. To remove this large mass, the dog underwent surgery on the upper part of its right front leg under general anaesthesia. The procedure began with standard preparation of the area, followed by the removal of the mass through blunt dissection after making an incision in the skin. The wound was closed by suturing the skin and underlying tissues separately. Parenteral antibiotics were administered for 5 days following the operation, and topical povidone‐iodine ointment was applied. After 10 days, the skin sutures and bandage were removed. The patient was monitored for 5 months, and there was no sign of the recurrence of the tumour; however, the patient died 5 months post‐operatively. The tumour was semi‐soft, fleshy, friable with irregular margins, almost round shape, a solid texture, and the mass had a mixed whitish‐pink colour (Figure 1).

caption.p: Mass excised from the right forelimb near elbow joint region.

sec.p: The excised mass was then referred to the histopathology laboratory at Department Pathology & Parasitology at Chattogram Veterinary & Animal Sciences University for examination for histopathological evaluation. The three representative tissue samples were taken from the mass, preserved in 10% formalin, processed routinely, and embedded in paraffin wax. Thin sections of paraffin‐embedded tissues were stained with haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) for examination under a light microscope.

sec.title: Result
sec.p: The cancer did not recur for 5 months while the patient was being monitored, but the patient died 5 months following surgery. The owner did not contact regularly, and for last 2 months, no imaging and blood testing were performed. After death, permission for post‐mortem examination was declined by the owner; therefore, the cause of death could not be determined.

The mass was semi‐soft, fleshy, friable, with uneven borders, an almost round form, and a solid texture, and it was a mixed whitish‐pink in colour. Following H&E staining of the tissue sections from the mass, it was examined under a light microscope at different magnifications. Under the microscope, the tissue was found to be consisted of numerous neoplastic mononuclear cells with prominent round‐to‐ovoid nuclei and scanty cytoplasm, showing signs of being poorly differentiated and having mild variations in nucleus size (anisokaryosis). These neoplastic cells were arranged in an alveolar pattern, forming nests separated by either thick or thin fibrous septa. They were unevenly distributed within the large and small alveolar spaces, either lining or covering the fibrous septa. Additionally, multiple mitotic figures were frequently observed in the less‐differentiated neoplastic cells, with four to five mitotic figures present per high‐power field (×100) (Figure 2).

caption.p: (A and B) Large clusters, nests, cords, and trabeculae of primitive round cells, separated by variably thick fibro‐vascular septa arranged in an alveolar pattern. (C) Small‐to‐intermediate monomorphic cells, spindle‐shaped rhabdomyoblast (RB) and a lot of mitotic figures (MF). (D) Neoplastic tumour cells characterized by small and large mononuclear cells with large round‐to‐ovoid hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm were separated by thick or thin fibrous septa and arranged in an alveolar pattern forming cell nests. Hematoxylin and Eosin stain (H&E stain).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: After surgery, the patient died 5 months later. There may be two possible explanations for this. It could be due to the dog's age; maybe the dog died biologically because it was an older dog. Indigenous dogs have an average life span of 8–15 years. So, it could have been a physiological death. Another possible cause could be death due to tumour metastasis; however, the lack of post‐mortem examination meant the tumour burden at death could not be defined. According to Caserto (2013), the metastasis rate of alveolar and embryonal RMS is about 50%, and in both cases, the survival rate is very poor. Murakami et al. (2010) reported that a dog died because of aggressive metastasis within 100 days while receiving radiation therapy and chemotherapy.

Alveolar RMS is characterized by a distinctive alveolar pattern in its tumour cells. These cells tend to form collagenous partitions and are often loosely grouped in the centre of the alveolar spaces. In poorly differentiated alveolar RMS, observing cross‐striations can be challenging (Park et al. 2016). The tumour itself is composed of two types of cells: poorly differentiated small cells arranged in a solid pattern and round‐to‐oval cells forming a characteristic alveolar pattern. In this alveolar pattern, cells are grouped into nests separated by fibrous septa, with a central loss of cellular cohesion leading to the creation of pseudo glandular spaces. While cells at the periphery remain attached to the fibrous septa, those in the centre tend to be more isolated and have eccentrically located hyperchromatic nuclei. Despite the absence of cross‐striations in the cancerous cells, the typical alveolar pattern strongly suggests a diagnosis of alveolar RMS (Sarnelli, Grassi, and Romagnoli 1994). In a case reported by Kimura et al. (2013), a 3‐year‐old Shih‐Tzu, a dog breed, had gingival alveolar RMS, with anaplastic cells arranged in a typical alveolar pattern, numerous mitotic figures, and multinucleated cells with no cross‐striations. In dogs, the solid variant of alveolar RMS may have thin fibrous septa dividing cell nests, resembling the classic ‘neuroendocrine pattern’, but often lacks this distinction, making it challenging to differentiate from rhabdomyoblastic embryonal RMS (Caserto 2013).

In this case, there was large clusters, nests, cords, and trabeculae of primitive round cells, separated by variably thick fibro‐vascular septa arranged in an alveolar pattern, small‐to‐intermediate monomorphic cells with multinucleated tumour giant cells, spindle‐shaped rhabdomyoblast, and several mitotic figures. Cells of origin for RMS could be mesenchymal progenitors, satellite cells, or endothelial progenitor cells. It is difficult to say since the cells are highly undifferentiated.

This study lacks immunohistochemistry for diagnosis, though it would be better if it could be included. Vimentin, desmin, myoglobin, and myogenin are important skeletal muscle immunohistochemistry markers for diagnosing RMS (Dias et al. 2000). Vimentin, a mesenchymal cell marker, is expressed in striated muscle during early differentiation stages (Yener 2001), while desmin is an intermediate filament protein and is found in adult muscles (Fletcher 2007). Myogenin, a myogenic transcriptional factor, induces myoblast differentiation into multinucleated myotubes. Its expression varies depending on the myogenesis stage and is intense during the late stages. After maturation, myogenin expression decreases, making it a useful marker for understanding canine RMS (Kobayashi et al. 2004).

There have been no reports of this type of cancer in indigenous dogs in Bangladesh. A relatively small amount of work has also been done on indigenous dogs in other countries. So far, this is the first report on ARMS in an indigenous dog in Bangladesh. Therefore, some challenges emerged when studying this case. However, ARMS is quite uncommon in other countries, with very low frequency and prevalence.

In this case, considering the observations made during the physical examination, based on the gross findings, microscopic findings, and the knowledge that a lack of cross‐striation is frequently observed in poorly differentiated RMS, this particular case, in the absence of immunohistochemical confirmation, is concluded to most likely be an alveolar RMS. It is noteworthy that despite the tumour's well‐contained and clearly defined appearance, the evidence still supports this diagnosis.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Canine RMS is a relatively rare and diagnostically challenging malignancy with several subtypes, including alveolar RMS, botryoid RMS, and embryonal RMS. Histopathological and immune‐histochemical examinations are essential in veterinary medicine for identifying RMS and screening out other neoplasms. RMS presents a challenge to pathologists and clinicians due to its wide range of variability, lack of prognostic value of its histologic subtypes, and the few numbers of documented occurrences in animals. The case of alveolar RMS in an indigenous dog in Bangladesh represents the prevalence of this type of cancer in this region. More research is required to better understand the biological behaviour and useful strategy for its classifications and characterization.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Monoar Sayeed Pallab and Farjana Akter: conceptualization, visualization, methodology, resources, supervision, investigation, formal analysis, writing‐original draft, writing‐review and editing. Md. Zillur Rahman, Mohammad Mahbubur Rahman, Bristy Dhar Nandita, Md. Foisal Shikder, and Md. Piar Rahaman Shihab: Investigation, resources, formal analysis.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: The authors have nothing to report.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Peer Review
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.70100.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors would like to thank the dog owner, Sahedul Alam Quaderi Teaching Veterinary Hospital (SAQTVH) for the assistance in collecting samples and data, and lab assistance of histopathology laboratory, Chattogram Veterinary and Animal Sciences University. Special thanks to respected Mohammad Mahbubur Rahman, professor, Department of Pathology and Parasitology, Chattogram Veterinary and Animal Sciences University and Dr. Zillur Rahman, professor, Department of Pathology, Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujib Medical University, for their sincere co‐operations in diagnosis of the case during this study.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Data dataset used or analyzed in this study are available to the corresponding author upon request.

ref.mixed-citation: Bae, I. H., Y.Kim, B.Pakhrin, et al. 2007. “Genitourinary Rhabdomyosarcoma With Systemic Metastasis in a Young Dog.” Veterinary Pathology44, no. 4: 518–520.1760651410.1354/vp.44-4-518

Fletcher, C. D.2007. “Soft Tissue Tumors.” In Diagnostic Histopathology of Tumors. Edinburgh, Churchill Livingstone, 1527–1592.

Kobayashi, M., H.Sakai, A.Hirata, et al. 2004. “Expression of Myogenic Regulating Factors, Myogenin and MyoD, in Two Canine Botryoid Rhabdomyosarcomas.” Veterinary Pathology41, no. 3: 275–277.1513317810.1354/vp.41-3-275

Murakami, M., H.Sakai, N.Iwatani, et al. 2010. “Cytologic, Histologic, and Immunohistochemical Features of Maxillofacial Alveolar Rhabdomyosarcoma in a Juvenile Dog.” Veterinary Clinical Pathology39, no. 1: 113–118.1964574210.1111/j.1939-165X.2009.00175.x
Parham, D. M.2001. “Pathologic Classification of Rhabdomyosarcomas and Correlations With Molecular Studies.” Modern Pathology14, no. 5: 506–514.1135306210.1038/modpathol.3880339
Park, J. K., E. M.Lee, A. Y.Kim, et al. 2016. “Alveolar Rhabdomyosarcoma in a Dog Confirmed Using Myogenin Immunohistochemistry: A Case Report.” Veterinární Medicína61, no. 5: 267–271.
Pîrvu, A. M., G. L.Nicolae, and M.Militaru. 2023. “Canine Rhabdomyosarcoma—Literature Review.” Scientific Works. Series C, Veterinary Medicine69, no. 1: 130–136.
Sarnelli, R., F.Grassi, and S.Romagnoli. 1994. “Alveolar Rhabdomyosarcoma of the Greater Omentum in a Dog.” Veterinary Pathology31, no. 4: 473–475.794123910.1177/030098589403100412
Yener, Z.2001. “Alveolar Rhabdomyosarcoma of the Limb in a Sheep.” Deutsche Tierarztliche Wochenschrift108, no. 7: 315–316.11505851
